sentences.text: Hiding.
Yes, that was in January, 1943.
And in January, as I said, all that period from September on, the ghetto was, as I said, not anymore a continuous living area.
But there were only those areas where the shops were.
And those were the areas where people worked.
And they were on the street outside mostly in the morning when they were to work.
And in the evening, when they went back to the home, if one can say home, to their apartments, or places where they lived.
And there was some-- they walked a bit around in the evening.
And those terrains-- those areas of the shops were really kind of labor camps.
That really was already a bit of a very much a labor camp.
And in January, there was this action.
And then, again, there was a period of relative quiet.

But we already must have-- my family must have planned before that the hope of salvation is in going on to the what was called Aryan side, Polish side, of Warsaw, and hiding, or getting false papers, or hiding otherwise, pretending that one was Polish, and leaving the ghetto.
And that was what-- I think that many, many people came to the same conclusion.

And all through that period, people were living, and leaving the ghetto, and obtaining false papers, and looking for a place to hide on the-- in the Aryan quarter of Warsaw, in the Aryan area of Warsaw, which was, of course, the whole of the city, except for the ghetto.
And it was a very hard decision, because although people were terribly afraid of staying in the ghetto, they were as well very much afraid of going to the Aryan side.
There was danger of being recognized, of being-- of being denounced to the Germans.
There were whole sections of Polish population who were practically living the different-- the lowest criminal layers of the society.
There were many who were now making their living out of finding the Jews who were hiding, of blackmailing them, of getting bribes or money out of them.
And in many cases, although they were-- they were getting money out of them, they still would give them to the Germans, or to the-- or to the Polish police.
So it was very dangerous.

And it was-- anyway, it was-- for the Poles, it was-- to keep Jews, or to try to save Jews, was as well terribly dangerous, because, really, from that point of view, if they found-- if the Germans found that Poles are sheltering Jews, then the Poles themselves face death as well, or at least going to Auschwitz-- so mostly death, I would say.
So it was a terribly difficult decision.
My family had some contacts on the Aryan side of Warsaw.
They heard that there was this Polish woman who was working as a house help, or maid, for my oldest uncle before the war.
And she was-- her name was Jula.
And she was very-- she was willing to help to save some family members.
And she was particularly keen to save the girl, the daughter of my uncle, Damka, who was the 16-year-old girl who was with me in one of those actions.
And she was now 16 or 17.
And Jula wanted to save her.
And-- but she was as well prepared to save, or at least to help to save, other people as well.
And, finally, it was-- somehow, my uncle Lulek, who was the manager of that shop, of that factory part, decided to take me out, and my mother as well.
And we were smuggled to the area inside of Warsaw in the beginning of February, '43, I think on the 8th of February.
And it was possible to get out of the ghetto.
It was physically-- it was not easy.
But it was possible.
You had, again, to bribe the police or the gendarme.

And they-- my uncle, who quite could officially leave to go to get some material for the factory, took me on the cart with him, and explained to the gendarme-- it was all fixed beforehand, of course-- that it's my birthday.
And he's going to get me a treat of some kind.
And so I was allowed to go out of ghetto on this cart.
And, of course, I never came back again.
And my mother left in the same way, I think a couple of days before me.
I don't remember it was couple days before or after.

And I then finished in the flat, first in some factory, where probably my uncle had to get these materials, these tissues-- not tissues, clothes, whatever it was that he needed, bales of cloth, or wool.
I don't know what it was anymore.
Then I stayed in this factory until it was dark.

And then I think Jula came, this woman came, and took me to her flat, to her tiny flat with one room and something, which was right next to the Umschlagplatz on the other side of the wall of course, but very near Umschlagplatz in a very not highly thought of part of Warsaw, with all kinds of-- with criminal elements living around, and lumpenproletariat, and so on.

Lumpenproletariat are people who-- what is now called in English under class, people who are not exactly fully employed, who are not workers, who live from hand to mouth, who are maybe stealing, or maybe doing some dealing in stolen things, or maybe doing some other doubtful things.
But, basically, have not-- or maybe working for a while, and then not a-- anyway, the lowest social class that exists, practically.
So lumpenproletariat now really made some of their income out of looking for Jews who are trying to hide.
And, indeed, only few days later, we were walking near Jula's flat somewhere with my mother and with Jula.

And I still must have been completely out of my mind, because after all, after spending three years or thereabouts, or two and a half years, in the ghetto, suddenly getting out of there, and being among normal people, so to say, was something completely incredible.
And I think that one said, if one could recognize-- one had to be able to-- on the Aryan side of Warsaw, you had to speak Polish perfectly.
You had to look like an Aryan.
You had to look like-- you couldn't have typical Jewish looks, because that was no good.
But even if you looked like an Aryan, I think that one was-- it was said that people were-- Jews were recognized by their eyes, because the eyes were-- because they had those frightened eyes.
Sorry?
And frightened expression.
Anyway, eyes, like animals that were being-- that were being hunted, practically.
So they, suddenly-- suddenly, there were teenagers who appeared next to us, and started screaming [POLISH], a little Jewess, because I was so covered.
They covered-- they wrapped me up in some clothes so that I think I looked-- they mistook me, apart from anything else, for a girl, because I was wrapped up in some cloak, of some coat.
So they mistook me for a girl.
And they put something on my head, because it was cold.
So my mother put-- probably wrapped up my head.
So they thought it was a Jewish girl.
Anyway, we managed to throw them off, somehow.
And we couldn't stay in Jula's place, because it was too dangerous.
So we managed to change into another flat.
And we changed the flats later on very regularly.
We changed them, and we changed them, because every time there was-- because living-- it was difficult to find a place to live.
And then after, if you found a place to live, after a few weeks, something was happening.
Either the flat owner decided that we were not-- not quite kosher, you know?
That we were not proper Poles, as one should be.
But there was something funny about us.
Or that there was suddenly danger of a blackmail.
So we first-- I think after Jula, we first moved to the [POLISH], to the castle square, which is in the very center of the old town of Warsaw.
And we lived there in a flat of some spinster lady, who was keeping us.
But she was terribly fearful.
And she was very nervous.
And we stayed there-- and nervous that we can destroy something of her things, because she was absolutely so crazy about everything that was in the flat.
And she was living on her own, you know?
So nothing ever got in.
I was only a boy.
So she was afraid that I might destroy some of her precious things.
And so we couldn't stay there very long.
We means you and your mother?
Me and my mother, yes, just two of us.
This, I don't know.
I didn't have any idea about money.
My mother was dealing with all the money business.
And my mother had still some money.
And she was, as well, trying to smuggle things, to buy food and sell food.
She was quite resourceful.
She was resourceful.
And she was, as well, a bit reckless, because she was still running around Warsaw without taking too much notice, and without-- without hiding herself.
And then-- and she was dragging me with her, because she, of course, didn't cook.
So we went from here and to there to eat.
And if we went somewhere, a cantina, or in a restaurant to eat more than twice, we wouldn't go there again, because it might be dangerous.
And then we changed again.
This lady in the castle square decided that we are too much of a risk.
So we moved out.
And we found a flat which, in with Mrs.
[NON-ENGLISH], whom I mentioned to you, in very much more posh area of Warsaw, in the-- near the filter stations.
That was a nice area of Warsaw, far from the center in the south, far from the ghetto.
And she had a little-- she was-- this lady, [NON-ENGLISH], the flat owner, she was, I think not a widow.
But she was married to an officer in the Polish army, a Major, who I think was in the west.
And she was there with her daughter in this flat.
But the flat was-- it was a flat built by the army before the war for families of the army people.
So it was a modern flat with three or four rooms.
And she kept-- and she rented to us one room.
And there was a mother with a daughter, some weird people living in another room.
And later, of course, I found out-- later, we found out that they were Jewish as well.
But we didn't know it in the beginning.
And we lived there then.
That must have been in March, April, '43.
And, as I said, my mother was a bit-- a bit-- she was not terribly careful.
She was walking here and there in Warsaw, just as if things were still not as bad as they were.
And I went with her.
And even she went for a-- maybe she was absolutely crazy going back to the ghetto, because my father was in the ghetto.
Most of her family members were still, some of her brothers and so on, and sisters, were still in the ghetto.
And all her friends were still in the ghetto.
So she wanted badly to go to the ghetto.
And she did.
She went.
She went to the ghetto.
It must have been in March, late March.
And she left me there on my own in this flat of [NON-ENGLISH].
How I stayed there on my own, [NON-ENGLISH] must have been-- I don't remember.
I think she was doing me something to eat.
She was quite nice.
But I stayed on my own there for maybe 10 days, or two weeks, you know?
We were-- we were then, you know, doing things which were, in retrospect might appear completely crazy.
Anyway, she came back from the ghetto.
And then-- and then it was already April.
Maybe she went in April already.
Maybe she-- because she must have come not long back, not long before the uprising started.
The uprising started on the 19th of April.
And, you know, for us it was being in the-- being in the Aryan quarter was, in a way, more frightening than being in the ghetto, because both were terribly frightening.
But at least-- but in the Aryan quarter, you were all the time surrounded by spies.
Every second could be your last.
You didn't know at all who is going to suddenly start blackmailing you, and where, and who?
One couldn't trust a soul.
And in ghetto, one still felt somehow-- among other Jews, one still felt safe, to an extent.
At least people, and family that were new, and so on.
So I can understand very well that she went back, and left me there for awhile.
Anyway, she came back.
And then the uprising started in April.
And--
The Jewish uprising, yes, and everybody knew about it, because there was immediately shooting, and fires, and artillery, and so on.
And we knew that still my father was there, and so on, and so forth.
So we were all in a state altogether.

And then this thing happened, which I mentioned to you before, that we went-- on one occasion, I went with my mother to eat lunch in that cantina in [POLISH] Street where we are there a few times before, and where the-- where one of the waitresses said to my mother that, your boy looks a bit-- he looks so unusual.
He looks like a Georgian.
So we didn't know exactly what she meant by that.
But we decided not to go there after that again.
Anyway, we went there on this occasion.
And then my mother went somewhere, God knows where then.
And I went on my own to the playground, to the children's playground, and was climbing over some climbing frames.
And, suddenly, those children started coming around me, Polish children who are there in the playground, and asking me questions.
And from fairly innocent questions, it changed into a complete interrogation.
And what was my name?
And where did I live?
And who was my father?
And who was my mother?
And I had, of course, answer-- although I didn't have false papers, but I had an answer to everything, because after all, we were already a bit prepared for such eventualities.
So I had a name, and a false name, and my father was an officer in a prisoner of war camp.
And my mother is living somewhere here, and went to the country to buy some food, and so on.
And, of course, I even knew all the prayers.
I knew all the Catholic prayers, et cetera, which I still know now, and everything.
So they were discussing-- there was a whole group of those teenagers who were older than I, because I was, after all, nine or nine and a half.
And they were maybe 12, 14, maybe even older.
And they were discussing if I was Jewish, or not Jewish, and if to call a policeman or not to call a policeman.
And, finally-- so I was withdrawing in the direction of the line-- the street car-- the tramway line.
And when the tramway came, I jumped onto the tram.
And they-- I think that they did it so that they didn't manage to jump onto that tram.
But I thought they might have jumped.
And then I thought they might have jumped to tram immediately following.
In short, I was so terrified that I-- first, I climbed under one of the benches, and was-- and then I got out.
And I was all the time for-- must have been for hours after, completely certain that they were following me, that they were-- I was in a state completely paranoid, thinking that they were behind me.
They were in a train behind.
They were searching.
They were somewhere here.
They were somewhere there.
And I didn't go out of the tram.
That that must have happened at maybe 3:00 or 4:00 in the afternoon, probably even earlier, because we ate lunch at 1:00 or so, maybe 3:00.
And I didn't leave the tram.
And the conductor didn't even ask me for a ticket.
And the tram was going from one end to another.
It must have taken every time three quarters of an hour or longer to go from one end of the line to another.
And so it went until 9:00 in the evening, when it was the police hour, curfew.
You couldn't after that move in Warsaw after 9:00.
If you moved in the street, you were dead.
They would shoot you.
So at 9:00, the tram came to the end stop at [NON-ENGLISH], which was not very far from where we lived, not very far from [NON-ENGLISH] flat, but not near enough to reach before the police hour.
And the policeman appeared in this tram.
And he started interrogating me as well.
Ask me, what are you doing here?
And why are you here?
And what is all that, and so on?
But somehow, the policeman was stupid.
And he didn't realize anything.
And he thought that I-- and I told him something, that I run away.
I told him a story that I had run away from home, that I got hiding from my father, that I did something naughty, that I run away, and that I was just afraid to go back home.
So he just told me to get out.
And I run into-- that was already the signal was there, already the siren for the police hour.
So I run to the first open gate of the house.
And I went there.
That was in this big block of flats.
And I spent all night on the steps, on the staircase, not sleeping, but getting up, and sitting on some landing.
And sometimes, people coming and complaining what I was doing there, and people going up the steps, and saying, [POLISH], which means, come up.
Let's see how the Jews are burning, how the little Jews are burning, you know?
Because they went up on the roof.
You saw the fires over the ghetto.
The ghetto was burning.
That was during the uprising.
That must have been late April.
And the uprising was in the ghetto.

And when-- in the first days of the uprising, Germans found out that it's too costly to try to go into the-- to try to clear people from the bunkers, and from the houses, and have fights with the resistance, and that they were having victims.
They decided an easier and quicker thing is to burn the place.
So they burned the place.
They set fire.
They set fire in a planned way to blocks of houses.
And they burned, and they burned, streets, and streets, and streets, all of the remaining ghetto.
And they had fire brigade on standby, that the fire doesn't come over to the Polish area.
So the fire brigade were along the wall, and were stopping the fires to come into the Polish area.
And otherwise, the houses where burning.
And people were often trapped int he houses.
And they were going-- sometimes they managed to get on down.
But then, of course, Germans were waiting for them, and shooting them down.
And sometimes, they were going up, and up, and up.
And sometimes, they managed to get into other houses which were not burning yet.
And very often, they didn't manage.
And then they were jumping out of the balconies, and throwing children out of the balconies, and jumping out of the roofs, and so on.
And that went on for a great part of April and May.
And the bunkers, of course-- and after everything was burnt, the Germans went for the bunkers.
And they were discovering the bunkers, sometimes with dogs, and sometimes with informers.
They were catching informers.
They were-- in other words, they were catching poor Jews.
And they were telling them, either you show us the bunkers--
Or you dead.
Or we shall shoot you on the spot.
And some people were prepared to show them the bunkers.
So they discovered the bunkers, either by informers, or by dogs, or by other means, or simply-- and they were getting people.
And they were throwing grenades, and gas grenades into the bunkers, and into the people that were going out, and getting them off still to Treblinka.

And so they finished everything in the-- but even after they finished with-- everything was burned, and all the bunkers, most of the bunkers, were uncovered, they still were not-- didn't rest until the whole of the ghetto, which was burnt, the burnt houses, were then exploded.

They had then-- they had then engineers, who were systematically exploding all of the remnants, the walls of the houses, which were still there, so that at the end of the war in late '44, of ghetto, apart from the prison, and the church, and a couple of other things, all this huge area was just like-- roughly like Sahara Desert, mounds of rubble, rubble.
There was nothing left except rubble.
And, of course, they discovered all of the bunkers that were there.
I don't see think any bunker-- any bunker survived.
